title: 01 - The Cell as a Unit of Health and Disease - Exam Prep Questions

chapter.title: The Cell as a Unit of Health and Disease

description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 1. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 1 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, tone, and difficulty distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format. Choices are keyed so answers remain stable if an application randomizes display order.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These questions are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.answer_key_note: Answers are included as stable choice keys with brief source-grounded rationales for study and review purposes.

source_notes.reference_note: Used as a style, structure, and difficulty reference.

questions.question:
A genome-wide association study identifies a variant in an enhancer region that increases the expression of a cytokine receptor without changing the amino acid sequence of the receptor.

What is the MOST accurate interpretation of this finding?

choices.text: A noncoding regulatory variant can alter disease susceptibility by changing gene expression
A synonymous exonic mutation is the most likely mechanism
Only mutations in protein-coding exons can influence phenotype
The variant must be a mitochondrial DNA mutation
The finding excludes an epigenetic contribution to disease

answer.reason: Noncoding enhancer variants can change transcription and disease susceptibility without altering the encoded amino acid sequence.

questions.question:
Two healthy individuals are found to differ at large contiguous stretches of genomic DNA rather than at isolated single bases.

What type of genomic variation is MOST likely being described?

answer.reason: CNVs are large gains, losses, or rearrangements of contiguous DNA rather than single-base variants.

questions.question:
A neutral SNP is repeatedly inherited with a nearby disease-causing allele because of physical proximity on the chromosome.

What term BEST describes this relationship?

answer.reason: A neutral SNP that is coinherited with a nearby causal allele because of physical proximity shows linkage disequilibrium.

questions.question:
Monozygotic twins have the same DNA sequence but develop different cellular phenotypes in several tissues.

What is the BEST explanation for this observation?

choices.text: Epigenetic modification causing altered gene expression

answer.reason: Cells with the same genome can develop different phenotypes when epigenetic changes alter gene expression without changing DNA sequence.

questions.question:
A drug increases histone acetylation at selected genomic regions.

What is the MOST likely immediate effect on chromatin at those sites?

choices.text: Chromatin opening with increased transcriptional accessibility
Immediate deletion of the targeted DNA sequence

answer.reason: Histone acetylation generally opens chromatin and increases transcriptional accessibility.

questions.question:
A female cell maintains physiologic silencing of one X chromosome through expression of XIST.

XIST is BEST classified as which of the following?

answer.reason: XIST is a long noncoding RNA that mediates physiologic X-chromosome inactivation.

questions.question:
A researcher introduces a short synthetic RNA into cultured cells to knock down expression of a chosen mRNA.

What molecule is being used?

answer.reason: siRNA works through the Dicer and RISC pathway to cleave target mRNA or repress its translation.

questions.question:
A pathogenic mutation is corrected experimentally by CRISPR/Cas9 in the presence of donor DNA carrying the desired normal sequence.

Which DNA repair pathway is required for this precise correction?

answer.reason: Precise CRISPR correction needs homology-directed repair when donor DNA is provided; nonhomologous end joining is error-prone.

questions.question:
A cell undergoing apoptosis displays an 'eat me' signal on its external membrane leaflet, promoting phagocytic clearance.

Which membrane phospholipid has most likely become externalized?

answer.reason: Phosphatidylserine normally remains on the inner leaflet and flips outward during apoptosis as an 'eat me' signal.

questions.question:
A toxin causes severe ATP depletion in a cell. Soon after, the cell becomes swollen and eventually ruptures.

What mechanism BEST explains the early swelling?

choices.text: Failure of ion-exchanging ATPases with osmotic water influx
Immediate activation of lysosomal hydrolases at the plasma membrane
Increased collagen synthesis in the extracellular matrix

answer.reason: ATP depletion disables ion pumps, so ions and water accumulate intracellularly and the cell swells osmotically.

questions.question:
A patient with familial hypercholesterolemia has impaired uptake of LDL into cells.

Which cellular process is primarily defective?

answer.reason: LDL uptake depends on clathrin-mediated receptor-mediated endocytosis, followed by endosomal release and receptor recycling.

questions.question:
A mutation disrupts claudin and occludin complexes in intestinal epithelium.

What cellular function would be MOST directly impaired?

choices.text: Mechanical linkage of intermediate filaments to the basement membrane
Paracellular barrier formation and maintenance of epithelial polarity

answer.reason: Claudin and occludin are tight-junction proteins that create the paracellular barrier and preserve epithelial polarity.

questions.question:
An undifferentiated malignant tumor stains strongly for cytokeratins.

What is the MOST likely lineage of origin?

answer.reason: Cytokeratins are intermediate filaments characteristic of epithelial cells and help identify epithelial tumors.

questions.question:
Cardiac muscle cells rapidly spread ions from one cell to another so the myocardium contracts as a coordinated syncytium.

Which junction is responsible for this?

answer.reason: Gap junctions allow ions and small molecules to pass directly between adjacent cells, coordinating cardiac contraction.

questions.question:
The most common cystic fibrosis mutation causes CFTR to misfold and be retained within the cell rather than reaching the plasma membrane.

In which organelle does this quality-control failure primarily occur?

answer.reason: Misfolded CFTR is retained during rough ER quality control instead of reaching the plasma membrane.

questions.question:
A lysosomal hydrolase is synthesized normally but fails to receive its targeting signal in the Golgi apparatus.

Which signal is missing?

answer.reason: Lysosomal hydrolases need mannose-6-phosphate tagging in the Golgi to be sorted to lysosomes.

questions.question:
A short-lived cytosolic transcription factor is polyubiquitinated and then degraded into peptide fragments.

Which intracellular structure mediates this degradation?

answer.reason: Proteasomes degrade ubiquitin-tagged cytosolic proteins, especially denatured proteins and short-lived regulatory proteins.

questions.question:
During nutrient deprivation, a cell surrounds senescent organelles with a double membrane and delivers them to lysosomes for degradation.

What process is being described?

answer.reason: Autophagy encloses senescent organelles in a double-membrane autophagosome before lysosomal degradation.

questions.question:
A pedigree shows transmission of a mitochondrial disorder only through affected mothers.

What is the BEST explanation for this pattern?

choices.text: Mitochondrial proteins are encoded only by the Y chromosome
Mitochondrial DNA is inherited almost entirely from the ovum
Mitochondrial genomes are replicated only during meiosis I
Mitochondria cannot undergo fission or fusion

answer.reason: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited almost entirely from the ovum, so transmission is maternal.

questions.question:
A rapidly proliferating malignant cell increases glucose and glutamine uptake, produces lactate despite adequate oxygen, and diverts intermediates into macromolecule synthesis.

What metabolic adaptation is this?

answer.reason: The Warburg effect is the shift toward glycolysis and biosynthetic intermediate production despite adequate oxygen.

questions.question:
Endothelial cells release nitric oxide, which diffuses into adjacent vascular smooth muscle and causes relaxation.

What intracellular second messenger is generated in the target smooth muscle cell?

answer.reason: Nitric oxide activates guanylyl cyclase in vascular smooth muscle, generating cGMP.

questions.question:
A mutation in RAS markedly delays hydrolysis of bound GTP.

What is the MOST likely consequence of this mutation?

choices.text: Loss of MAPK signaling with reduced proliferation
Persistent proliferative signaling downstream of growth factor receptors
Termination of signal transduction at the adaptor protein level

answer.reason: RAS stays active when GTP hydrolysis is delayed, prolonging downstream proliferative signaling.

questions.question:
A developmental signaling pathway is activated when a ligand on one cell binds a receptor on an adjacent cell, causing proteolytic cleavage of the receptor and nuclear translocation of the released intracellular fragment.

Which pathway is being described?

answer.reason: Notch signaling involves cell-to-cell ligand binding, receptor cleavage, and nuclear entry of the released intracellular fragment.

questions.question:
A healing wound shows capillary sprouting and increased vascular permeability in the surrounding tissue.

Which growth factor is MOST directly responsible for these changes?

answer.reason: VEGF is the major driver of angiogenesis and also increases vascular permeability.

questions.question:
A patient forms dense scar tissue after chronic injury. The dominant cytokine signal promotes extracellular matrix synthesis and suppresses acute inflammation through Smad-dependent signaling.

Which mediator BEST fits this description?

answer.reason: TGF-beta signals through Smads to promote extracellular matrix production and suppress acute inflammation.

questions.question:
A researcher wants to assess the integrity of the basement membrane beneath an epithelium.

Which extracellular matrix component is the MOST characteristic adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane?

answer.reason: Laminin is the dominant adhesive glycoprotein of basement membrane and links cells to the basement membrane matrix.

questions.question:
A tall young adult with lens dislocation and aortic root disease is found to have Marfan syndrome.

Deficiency of which extracellular matrix component BEST explains this disorder?

answer.reason: Marfan syndrome results from a fibrillin defect that disrupts elastic tissue architecture.

questions.question:
Integrins attach cells to extracellular matrix proteins such as fibronectin.

What peptide motif on the matrix protein is classically recognized by many integrins?

answer.reason: Many integrins recognize the RGD motif on extracellular matrix proteins such as fibronectin.

questions.question:
A proliferating cell receives growth factor stimulation and passes from G1 into S phase.

Which event is MOST important for this transition?

choices.text: Phosphorylation of Rb by cyclin D/E-dependent CDKs

answer.reason: Passage from G1 to S depends on cyclin D and cyclin E dependent CDKs phosphorylating Rb.

questions.question:
Fibroblasts from a patient are reprogrammed by introducing genes that restore stem-cell properties, after which the cells can be differentiated into multiple lineages in culture.

What has been generated?

answer.reason: Induced pluripotent stem cells are reprogrammed somatic cells with restored pluripotent, embryonic stem cell-like properties.
